paper_title: Poisoning Attacks against Support Vector Machines
core_contribution: A gradient ascent-based poisoning attack method that crafts malicious training points to maximize SVM test error. The method uses properties of the SVM's optimal solution to predict how adding attack points changes the decision function, and can be kernelized to work in input space even for non-linear kernels.
algorithm_steps: Step 1: Initialize attack point by cloning a point from attacked class and flipping its label
Step 2: Train SVM on training data plus current attack point using incremental SVM
Step 3: Compute gradient of validation loss with respect to attack point direction using Eq. (10)
Step 4: Set attack direction u to unit vector aligned with gradient
Step 5: Update attack point: x_c^(p) = x_c^(p-1) + t*u with fixed step size t
Step 6: Repeat until validation error change falls below threshold
key_equations: Eq. (1): Objective function maximizing hinge loss on validation set
Eq. (2): Margin conditions accounting for attack point influence
Eq. (7): Adiabatic update condition maintaining optimal SVM solution structure
Eq. (9): Gradients of dual variables and bias with respect to attack direction
Eq. (10): Final gradient expression for objective function optimization

key_assumptions.assumption: Attacker knows the learning algorithm (SVM) and training data
key_assumptions.why_needed: Required to compute gradients and predict SVM solution changes
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When attacker has limited knowledge of training data or algorithm parameters

key_assumptions.assumption: SVM support vector sets (S, E, R) remain unchanged during attack point updates
key_assumptions.why_needed: Enables smooth gradient computation and adiabatic update conditions
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When large step sizes cause support vector set structure to change

key_assumptions.assumption: Attack point can be drawn from same distribution as training data
key_assumptions.why_needed: Ensures attack points are realistic and not easily detectable
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When attack points must satisfy strict domain constraints or detection mechanisms exist

baseline_limitations_identified: Previous poisoning attacks were limited to simple anomaly detection methods and could not be constructed in input space for non-linear kernels
proposed_improvement: Kernelizable gradient-based approach that works in input space even for non-linear kernels, using SVM optimal solution properties
condition_where_baseline_wins: When the SVM uses very small training sets where support vector structure changes frequently, making gradient approximations unreliable
toy_dataset_justification: These datasets allow clear visualization of decision boundaries and attack point effects, have simple structure for debugging gradient computations, and work with different kernel types
reproducibility_notes: Requires implementing incremental SVM updates and careful handling of gradient computations with different kernels. Step size selection and convergence criteria need tuning.

key_components_for_ablation.component: Adiabatic update condition maintaining support vector structure
key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Enables smooth gradient computation by assuming SVM solution structure remains constant
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would require recomputing full SVM solution at each step, making method computationally expensive

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Allows attack construction in input space for non-linear kernels
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would limit method to linear kernels or require feature space construction

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Simplifies optimization by avoiding expensive line search
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Could improve convergence but increase computational cost significantly

known_failure_modes.scenario: When attack point becomes a reserve point (alpha_i = 0)
known_failure_modes.reason: Reserve points don't contribute to decision function, halting attack progress
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Attack point initialization must be sufficiently deep within attacking class margin

known_failure_modes.scenario: When step size is too large causing support vector structure changes
known_failure_modes.reason: Violates adiabatic update assumption, making gradient computations invalid
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: SVM support vector sets remain unchanged during updates

known_failure_modes.scenario: When validation and training data distributions differ significantly
known_failure_modes.reason: Attack optimized on validation set may not transfer to actual test performance
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Validation set represents true test distribution
